content: The general solutions of the equation $\tan ^2 \theta+\sec 2 \theta=1$ are
explanation:
Using $\sec 2 \theta=\frac{1}{\cos 2 \theta}=\frac{1+\tan ^2 \theta}{1-\tan ^2 \theta}$, we can write the given equation as

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan ^2 \theta+\frac{1+\tan ^2 \theta}{1-\tan ^2 \theta}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^2 \theta\left(1-\tan ^2 \theta\right)+1+\tan ^2 \theta=1-\tan ^2 \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \tan ^2 \theta-\tan ^4 \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^2 \theta\left(3-\tan ^2 \theta\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=0 \text { or } \tan ^2 \theta=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=0 \text { or } \tan ^2 \theta=\tan ^2 \frac{\pi}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi \text { or } \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3}, \text { where } \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\tan (\pi \cos \theta)=\cot (\pi \sin \theta)$, then $\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right)=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan (\pi \cos \theta)=\cot (\pi \sin \theta) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin (\pi \cos \theta)}{\cos (\pi \cos \theta)}=\frac{\cos (\pi \sin \theta)}{\sin (\pi \sin \theta)} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos (\pi \cos \theta) \cos (\pi \sin \theta) -\sin (\pi \cos \theta) \sin (\pi \sin \theta)=0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cos (\pi \cos \theta+\pi \sin \theta)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \pi \cos \theta+\pi \sin \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta+\sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \theta+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \theta\right)=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right)=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} $$

content: The common principal solution of the equations $\sin \theta=-\frac{1}{2}$ and $\tan \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ is
explanation:
$\sin \theta=\frac{-1}{2}$ and $\tan \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$\Rightarrow \sin \theta=-\sin \frac{\pi}{6}$ and $\tan \theta=\tan \frac{\pi}{6}$

$\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\sin \left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ and $\tan \theta=\tan \left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$\Rightarrow$ common principal solution $=\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{7 \pi}{6}$

content: The principal solution of of $(5+3 \sin \theta)(2 \cos \theta+1)=0$ are
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & (5+3 \sin \theta)(2 \cos \theta+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{-5}{3}, \text { which is not possible } \\ & \text { or } \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3} \text { and } \cos \theta=\cos \frac{4 \pi}{3} \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { The principal solutions are } \frac{2 \pi}{3} \text { and } \frac{4 \pi}{3} \text {. } \end{aligned} $$

content: The number of values of $x$ in the interval $[0,3 \pi]$ satisfying the equation $2 \sin ^2 x+5 \sin x-3=0$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & 2 \sin ^2 x+5 \sin x-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin ^2 x+6 \sin x-\sin x-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin x(\sin x+3)-1(\sin x+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+3)(2 \sin x-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x=-3, \text { which is not possible } \\ & \text { or } \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x=\sin \frac{\pi}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^n \frac{\pi}{6}, \mathrm{n} \in Z \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}, \frac{13 \pi}{6}, \frac{17 \pi}{6} \quad \ldots[x \in(0,3 \pi)] \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Number of values of } x=4 \text {. } \end{aligned} $$

content: The number of values of $x$ in the interval $[0,3 \pi]$ satisfying the equation $2 \sin ^2 x+5 \sin x-3=0$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \sin ^2 x+5 \sin x-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin ^2 x+6 \sin x-\sin x-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin x(\sin x+3)-1(\sin x+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(2 \sin x-1)(\sin x+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \text { or }-3 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \quad \ldots[\because \sin x \neq-3] \\ \therefore & x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}, \frac{13 \pi}{6}, \frac{17 \pi}{6} \quad \ldots[x \in[0,3 \pi]] \\ \therefore & \text { Number of solutions }=4 \end{array} $$

content: If $\frac{1}{6} \sin \theta, \cos \theta, \tan \theta$ are in G.P., then the general solution of $\theta$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since } \frac{1}{6} \sin \theta, \cos \theta, \tan \theta \text { are in G.P. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{1}{6} \sin \theta \cdot \tan \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{\sin ^2 \theta}{6 \cos \theta} \\ & \Rightarrow 6 \cos ^3 \theta=\sin ^2 \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 6 \cos ^3 \theta=1-\cos ^2 \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 6 \cos ^3 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\cos \theta-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(6 \cos ^2 \theta+4 \cos \theta+2\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The number of solutions of $16^{\sin ^2 x}+16^{\cos ^2 x}=10$ in $0 \leqslant x \leqslant 2 \pi$ are
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & 16^{\sin ^2 x}+16^{\cos ^2 x}=10 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { Let } y=16^{\sin ^2 x} \\ \therefore \quad & 16^{\cos ^2 x}=16^{\left(1-\sin ^2 x\right)}=\frac{16}{16^{\sin ^2 x}}=\frac{16}{y} \end{array} $$

Equation (i) becomes

$$ \begin{array}{llll} & y+\frac{16}{y}=10 & & \\ & \Rightarrow y^2-10 y+16=0 & \\\ \Rightarrow &y=8 & \text { or } & y=2 \\ \therefore & 16^{\sin ^2 x}=8 & \text { or } & 16^{\sin ^2 x}=2 \\ & 2^{4 \sin ^2 x}=2^3 & \text { or } & 2^{4 \sin ^2 x}=2^1 \\ \Rightarrow & 4 \sin ^2 x=3 & \text { or } & 4 \sin ^2 x=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \sin ^2 x=\frac{3}{4} & \text { or } & \sin ^2 x=\frac{1}{4} \\ \Rightarrow & \sin x=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & \text { or } & \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \\ & x=\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}, \frac{5 \pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}, \frac{7 \pi}{6}, \frac{11 \pi}{6} & \end{array} $$

Required number of roots $=8$

content: If $\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cot \theta\right)=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \tan \theta\right)$, then the general solution of $\theta$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cot \theta\right)=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \tan \theta\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cot \theta\right)=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4} \tan \theta\right) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \ldots\left(\because \cos \theta=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4} \cot \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4} \tan \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}(\cot \theta+\tan \theta)=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cot \theta+\tan \theta=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\tan \theta}+\tan \theta=2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots\left(\because \cot \theta=\frac{1}{\tan \theta}\right)\\ & \Rightarrow 1+\tan ^2 \theta=2 \tan \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 1-2 \tan \theta+\tan ^2 \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(1-\tan \theta)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\tan \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{\pi}{4},>\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{z} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $3 \sin 2 \theta=2 \sin 3 \theta$ and $0<\theta<\pi$, then the value of $\sin \theta$ is equal to
explanation:
Given, $3 \sin 2 \theta=2 \sin 3 \theta, 0<\theta<\pi$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 3(2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)=2\left(3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^3 \theta\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 6 \sin \theta \cos \theta=6 \sin \theta-8 \sin ^3 \theta \end{aligned} $$

Dividing both sides by $\sin \theta$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 6 \cos \theta=6-8 \sin ^2 \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 6 \cos \theta=6-8\left(1-\cos ^2 \theta\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 8 \cos ^2 \theta-6 \cos \theta-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \cos ^2 \theta-3 \cos \theta-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\cos \theta-1)(4 \cos \theta+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=1 \text { or } \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{4} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \quad \operatorname{But} 0 & <\theta<\pi \\ \therefore \quad \cos \theta & \neq 1 \\ \therefore \quad \cos \theta & =\frac{-1}{4} \\ & \\ & \sin ^2 \theta=1-\cos ^2 \theta \\ & =1-\left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \sin ^2 \theta=\frac{15}{16} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4} \quad \ldots[0<\theta<\pi] \end{aligned} $$

content: The possible values of $\theta \in(0, \pi)$ such that $\sin \theta+\sin (4 \theta)+\sin (7 \theta)=0$ are
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \sin 7 \theta+\sin \theta+\sin 4 \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin 4 \theta \cos 3 \theta+\sin 4 \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta(2 \cos 3 \theta+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta=0 \quad \text { or } \quad \cos 3 \theta=\frac{-1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta=0 \quad \text { or } \quad \cos 3 \theta=\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \theta=n \pi \quad \text { or } \quad 3 \theta=2 n \pi \pm \frac{2 \pi}{3} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{lll} & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{4} \quad \text { or } & \theta=\frac{2 \mathrm{n} \pi}{3} \pm \frac{2 \pi}{9} \\ \therefore & \theta=\frac{2 \pi}{9}, \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{4 \pi}{9}, \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{8 \pi}{9} & \ldots[\because \theta \in(0, \pi)] \end{array} $$

content: If $\tan 3 \theta=\cot \theta$, then $\theta=$
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & \tan 3 \theta=\cot \theta \\ \therefore & \tan 3 \theta=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right) \\ \therefore & 3 \theta=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta, n \in Z \\ \therefore & 4 \theta=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \therefore & \theta=\frac{(2 n+1) \pi}{8}, n \in Z \end{array} $$

content: The general solution of the equation $\sqrt{3} \cos \theta+\sin \theta=\sqrt{2}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{3} \cos \theta+\sin \theta=\sqrt{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos \theta+\frac{1}{2} \sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \frac{\pi}{3} \cos \theta+\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \left(\theta+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\sin \frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta+\frac{\pi}{3}=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\pi}{4}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{\pi}{3}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $P=\{\theta / \sin \theta-\cos \theta=\sqrt{2} \cos \theta\}$ and $Q=\{\theta / \sin \theta+\cos \theta=\sqrt{2} \sin \theta\}$ be two sets, then
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \sin \theta-\cos \theta=\sqrt{2} \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=(\sqrt{2}+1) \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{2}+1} \times \frac{\sqrt{2}-1}{\sqrt{2}-1}=\cos \theta \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \theta(\sqrt{2}-1)}{(\sqrt{2})^2-1^2}=\cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \theta(\sqrt{2}-1)}{2-1}=\cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{2} \sin \theta-\sin \theta=\cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta+\cos \theta=\sqrt{2} \sin \theta \\ \therefore \quad & P=Q \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of values of $x$ in the interval $(0,5 \pi)$ satisfying the equation $3 \sin ^2 x-7 \sin x+2=0$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 3 \sin ^2 x-7 \sin x+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \sin x-6 \sin x-\sin x+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \sin x(\sin x-2)-(\sin x-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(3 \sin x-1)(\sin x-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{3} \text { or } 2\\ & \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{3} \quad \ldots .[\because \sin x \neq 2] \end{aligned}$$

Let $\sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}=\alpha, 0<\alpha<\frac{\pi}{2}$ are the solutions in $[0,5 \pi]$. Then, $\alpha, \pi-\alpha, 2 \pi+\alpha, 3 \pi-\alpha$, $4 \pi+\alpha, 5 \pi-\alpha$ are the solutions in [0,5 $5 \pi$.

$\therefore\quad$ number of solutions $=6$

content: The solution set of the equation $\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x$, in the interval $[0,2 \pi]$ is
explanation:
The given equation is defined for $x \neq \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}$.

Now, $\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}+\frac{1}{\cos x}=2 \cos x$

$\Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2 \cos ^2 x$

$\Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2\left(1-\sin ^2 x\right)$

$\Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2(1-\sin x)(1+\sin x)$

$\Rightarrow(1+\sin x)[2(1-\sin x)-1]=0$

$\Rightarrow 2(1-\sin x)-1=0$

$\ldots\left[\begin{array}{r}\because \sin x \neq-1 \text { otherwise } \cos x=0 \text { and } \\ \tan x, \sec x \text { will be undefined }\end{array}\right]$

$\Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{2}$

$\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}$ in $(0,2 \pi)$

content: The number of integral values of k for which the equation $7\cos x+5\sin x=2k+1$ has a solution, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & -\sqrt{7^2+5^2} \leq(7 \cos x+5 \sin x) \leq \sqrt{7^2+5^2} \\\\ & \Rightarrow-\sqrt{74} \leq(7 \cos x+5 \sin x) \leq \sqrt{74} \\\\ & \Rightarrow-8.6 \leq 2 k+1 \leq 8.6 \\\\ & \Rightarrow-4.8 \leq k \leq 3.8 \end{aligned}$$

Integral values of k are $-4,-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3$

Number of integral values of $k=8$

content: Let $a, b, c$ be three non-zero real numbers such that the equation $\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a} \cos x+2 b \sin x=c$, $x \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ has two distinct real roots $\alpha$ and $\beta$ with $\alpha+\beta=\frac{\pi}{3}$. Then the value of $\frac{b}{a}$ is
explanation:
$\alpha$ and $\beta$ are roots of $\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a} \cos x+2 \mathrm{~b} \sin x=\mathrm{c}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \sqrt{3} a \cos \alpha+2 b \sin \alpha=c \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \sqrt{3} a \cos \beta+2 b \sin \beta=c \quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Subtracting (ii) from (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{3} a(\cos \alpha-\cos \beta)+2 b(\sin \alpha-\sin \beta)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} a\left[-2 \sin \left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)\right] \\ & +2 b\left[2 \cos \left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)\right]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\sqrt{3}\left[2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)\right] \\ & +2 b\left[2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)\right]=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-\sqrt{3} a \sin \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)+2 b\left[\sqrt{3} \sin \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)\right]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\sqrt{3} a+2 \sqrt{3} b=0 \Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=\frac{1}{2}=0.5 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the equation $\cos ^4 \theta+\sin ^4 \theta+\lambda=0$ has real solutions for $\theta$, then $\lambda$ lies in the interval
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \cos ^4 \theta+\sin ^4 \theta+\lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\sin ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta\right)^2-2 \sin ^2 \theta \cos ^2 \theta+\lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1-2 \sin ^2 \theta \cos ^2 \theta+\lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=2 \sin ^2 \theta \cos ^2 \theta-1 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{\sin ^2 2 \theta}{2}-1 \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Since }-1 \leq \sin 2 \theta \leq 1, \\ & 0 \leq \sin ^2 2 \theta \leq 1 \\ & \Rightarrow 0 \leq \frac{\sin ^2 2 \theta}{2} \leq \frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow-1 \leq \frac{\sin ^2 2 \theta}{2}-1 \leq-\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda \in\left[-1,-\frac{1}{2}\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of $\sin x+\cos x=1$ is
explanation:
$$\cos x+\sin x=1$$

Dividing both sides by $\sqrt{2}$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \therefore \quad & \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \cos x+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \sin x=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Since } \cos \theta=\cos \alpha \text { implies } \theta=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \pm \alpha, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z} \text {. }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & x-\frac{\pi}{4}=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{4} \\ \therefore & x=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{\pi}{4} \\ \therefore & x=2 \mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{\pi}{4} \text { or } \quad x=2 \mathrm{n} \pi-\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{\pi}{4} \\ \therefore & x=2 \mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{\pi}{2} \quad \text { or } \quad x=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The required general solution is $x=2 \mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}$ or $2 \mathrm{n} \pi$, where $n \in \mathrm{Z}$.

content: The number of solutions of $\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x$ in $[0,2 \pi]$ is
explanation:
The given equation is defined for $x \neq \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}$.

Now, $\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}+\frac{1}{\cos x}=2 \cos x \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2 \cos ^2 x \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2\left(1-\sin ^2 x\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2(1-\sin x)(1+\sin x) \\ & \Rightarrow(1+\sin x)[2(1-\sin x)-1]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(1-\sin x)-1=0 \\ & \ldots\left[\begin{array}{r} \because \sin x \neq-1 \text { otherwise } \cos x=0 \text { and } \\ \tan x, \sec x \text { will be undefined } \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6} \text { in }[0,2 \pi] \\ & \therefore \quad \text { number of solutions }=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If angle $\theta$ in $[0,2 \pi]$ satisfies both the equations $\cot \theta=\sqrt{3}$ and $\sqrt{3} \sec \theta+2=0$, then $\theta$ is equal to
explanation:
$\cot \theta=\sqrt{3}$ and $\sec \theta=\frac{-2}{\sqrt{3}}$

i.e., $\tan \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ and $\cos \theta=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

$\therefore \quad \theta$ lies in $3^{\text {rd }}$ quadrant

$\therefore \quad \theta=\frac{7 \pi}{6}$

content: If for certain $x, 3 \cos x \neq 2 \sin x$, then the general solution of, $\sin ^2 x-\cos 2 x=2-\sin 2 x$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } \\ & \sin ^2 x-\cos 2 x=2-\sin 2 x \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\cos ^2 x-\left(2 \cos ^2 x-1\right)-2+\sin 2 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1-3 \cos ^2 x+1-2+2 \sin x \cdot \cos x \\ & \Rightarrow \cos x(2 \sin x-3 \cos x)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos x=0 \text { or } 2 \sin x-3 \cos x=0 \\ & \text { But } 2 \sin x \neq 3 \cos x \\ & \Rightarrow \cos x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{2}, n \in Z \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $2 \sin ^2 x+3 \sin x-2>0$ and $x^2-x-2<0$ ($x$ is measured in radians). Then $x$ lies in the interval
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 2 \sin ^2 x+3 \sin x-2>0 \\ & (2 \sin x-1)(\sin x+2)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin x-1>0 \ldots[\because \sin x+2>0 \text { and } x \in \mathrm{R}] \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x>\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Also, } x^2-x-2<0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-2)(x+1)<0 \\ & \Rightarrow-1< x<2 \end{aligned}$$

Since $2<\frac{5 \pi}{6}$

$\therefore \quad x$ must lie in $\left(\frac{\pi}{6}, 2\right)$

content: The number of integral values of $k$, for which the equation $7 \cos x+5 \sin x=2 \mathrm{k}+1$ has a solution, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & -\sqrt{7^2+5^2} \leq(7 \cos x+5 \sin x) \leq \sqrt{7^2+5^2} \\ & \Rightarrow-\sqrt{74} \leq(7 \cos x+5 \sin x) \leq \sqrt{74} \\ & \Rightarrow-8.6 \leq 2 k+1 \leq 8.6 \\ & \Rightarrow-4.8 \leq k \leq 3.8 \end{aligned}$$

Integral values of k are $-4,-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3$

Number of integral values of $k=8$

content: The smallest positive value of $x$ in degrees satisfying the equation $\tan \left(x+100^{\circ}\right)=\tan \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \tan (x) \tan \left(x-50^{\circ}\right)$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \tan \left(x+100^{\circ}\right)=\tan \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \tan (x) \tan \left(x-50^{\circ}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\tan \left(x+100^{\circ}\right)}{\tan \left(x-50^{\circ}\right)}=\tan \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \tan (x) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 \sin \left(x+100^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(x-50^{\circ}\right)}{2 \cos \left(x+100^{\circ}\right) \sin \left(x-50^{\circ}\right)} \\ & =\frac{2 \sin \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \sin x}{2 \cos \left(x+50^{\circ}\right) \cos x} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin (2 x+50)+\sin 150^{\circ}}{\sin (2 x+50)-\sin 150^{\circ}} \\ &=\frac{\cos \left(50^{\circ}\right)-\cos \left(2 x+50^{\circ}\right)}{\cos \left(2 x+50^{\circ}\right)+\cos 50^{\circ}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { By componendo-dividendo, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{2 \sin \left(2 x+50^{\circ}\right)}{2 \sin \left(150^{\circ}\right)}=\frac{2 \cos 50^{\circ}}{-2 \cos \left(2 x+50^{\circ}\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin \left(2 x+50^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(2 x+50^{\circ}\right) \\ & \quad=-2 \sin \left(150^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(50^{\circ}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sin \left(4 x+100^{\circ}\right)=-\cos 50^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \left(4 x+100^{\circ}\right)=\sin \left(270^{\circ}-50^{\circ}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 4 x+100^{\circ}=220^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow 4 x=120^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow x=30^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of solutions, of $2^{1+|\cos x|+|\cos x|^2+\ldots \ldots \cdots \cdots}=4$ in $(-\pi, \pi)$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 2^{1+|\cos x|+|\cos x|^2+\ldots}=4 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^{\frac{1}{1-|\cos x|}}=2^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{1-|\cos x|}=2 \\ & \Rightarrow 1-|\cos x|=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow|\cos x|=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos x= \pm \frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{-2 \pi}{3}, \frac{-\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3} \quad \ldots[\because x \in(-\pi, \pi)] \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ Number of solutions = 4

content: In $(0,2 \pi)$, the number of solutions of $\tan \theta+\sec \theta=2 \cos \theta$ are
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \tan \theta+\sec \theta=2 \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}+\frac{1}{\cos \theta}=2 \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta+1=2 \cos ^2 \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta+1=2\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin ^2 \theta+\sin \theta-1=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \sin \theta=-1 & , \frac{1}{2} \\ \text { For } \sin \theta & =-1, \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ \theta & =\frac{3 \pi}{2} \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6} \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Number of solutions }=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of $\sin x-3 \sin 2 x+\sin 3 x=\cos x-3 \cos 2 x+\cos 3 x$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \sin x-3 \sin 2 x+\sin 3 x \\ & =\cos x-3 \cos 2 x+\cos 3 x \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+\sin 3 x)-3 \sin 2 x-(\cos x+\cos 3 x) \\ & +3 \cos 2 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin 2 x \cos x-3 \sin 2 x-2 \cos 2 x \cos x \\ & +3 \cos 2 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin 2 x(2 \cos x-3)-\cos 2 x(2 \cos x-3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin 2 x-\cos 2 x)(2 \cos x-3)=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$\Rightarrow \cos 2 x=\sin 2 x \quad \ldots\left[\because \cos x \neq \frac{3}{2}\right]$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cos 2 x=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-2 x\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x=2 n \pi \pm\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-2 x\right) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Neglecting }(-) \text { sign, we get }\\ &x=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{8} \end{aligned}$$

content: The Solution set of the equation $\sin ^2 \theta-\cos \theta=\frac{1}{4}$ in the interval $[0,2 \pi]$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \sin ^2 \theta-\cos \theta=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \left(1-\cos ^2 \theta\right)-\cos \theta=\frac{1}{4} \\ & 4-4 \cos ^2 \theta-4 \cos \theta-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \cos ^2 \theta+4 \cos \theta-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{2} \text { or } \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & \theta=2 \pi-\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{3} \\ \therefore \quad & \theta=\left\{\frac{5 \pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{3}\right\} \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of all values of $\theta$ in the interval $\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ satisfying the equation $(1-\tan \theta)(1+\tan \theta) \sec ^2 \theta+2 \tan ^2 \theta=0$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & (1-\tan \theta)(1+\tan \theta) \sec ^2 \theta+2 \tan ^2 \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(1-\tan ^2 \theta\right)\left(1+\tan ^2 \theta\right)+2 \tan ^2 \theta=0 \end{aligned}$$

Put $\tan ^2 \theta=x$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Put }(1-x)(1+x)+2 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(1-x^2+2 x=0\right. \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-1=2 x \end{aligned}$$

Let us draw graph of $y=x^2-1$ and $y=2 x$

From the graph the two curves are intersecting at 2 points.

$\therefore \quad$ There are 2 values of $x$.

Only one value of $x$ exists, for $\theta \in\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

$\therefore \quad x=\tan ^2 \theta$

$\therefore \quad$ Two values of $\theta$ satisfies above equation

content: Let $S=\left\{x \in(-\pi, \pi) \mid x \neq 0, \pm \frac{\pi}{2}\right\}$. The sum of all distinct solutions of the equation $\sqrt{3} \sec x+\operatorname{cosec} x+2(\tan x-\cot x)=0$ in the set S is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{3} \sec x+\operatorname{cosec} x+2(\tan x-\cot x)=0 \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sec x+\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{cosec} x=\cot x-\tan x \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{1}{\cos x}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{\sin x}=\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}-\frac{\sin x}{\cos x} \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin x+\frac{1}{2} \cos x=\cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x \\ \therefore\quad& \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}-x\right)=\cos 2 x \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{\pi}{3}-x=2 n \pi \pm 2 x \end{aligned}$$

for $n=0: x=\frac{\pi}{9} \in(-\pi, \pi)$ or $\frac{-\pi}{3} \in(-\pi, \pi)$

for $\mathrm{n}=1: x=\frac{-5 \pi}{9} \in(-\pi, \pi)$ or $x=\frac{5 \pi}{3} \notin(-\pi, \pi)$

for $\mathrm{n}=-1: x=\frac{-7 \pi}{3} \notin(-\pi, \pi)$ or $x=\frac{7 \pi}{9} \in(-\pi, \pi)$

for $\mathrm{n}=2: x=\frac{11 \pi}{3} \notin(-\pi, \pi)$ or $x=\frac{-11 \pi}{9} \notin(-\pi, \pi)$

$\therefore\quad$ Distinct solutions are : $\frac{\pi}{9}, \frac{-\pi}{3}, \frac{-5 \pi}{9}, \frac{7 \pi}{9}$

$\therefore\quad$ Required sum $=\frac{\pi-3 \pi-5 \pi+7 \pi}{9}=0$

content: The number of roots of the equation, $(81)^{\sin ^2 x}+(81)^{\cos ^2 x}=30$ in the interval $[0, \pi]$, is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &(81)^{\sin ^2 x}+(81)^{\cos ^2 x}=30\quad\text{.... (i)}\\ &\text { Let } y=81^{\sin ^2 x}\\ \therefore\quad&81^{\cos ^2 x}=81^{\left(1-\sin ^2 x\right)}=\frac{81}{81^{\sin ^2 x}}=\frac{81}{y} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation (i) becomes

$$\begin{aligned} & y+\frac{81}{y}=30 \\ \therefore \quad & y^2-30 y+81=0 \\ \therefore \quad & (y-27)(y-3)=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad y=27 \text { or } 3$$

$\therefore \quad 81^{\sin ^2 x}=27 \quad$ or $\quad 81^{\sin ^2 x}=3$

$\therefore \quad 3^{4 \sin ^2 x}=3^3 \quad$ or $\quad 3^{4 \sin ^2 x}=3^1$

$\therefore \quad 4 \sin ^2 x=3 \quad$ or $\quad 4 \sin ^2 x=1$

$\therefore \quad \sin x=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{2}$ $\ldots[\because x \in[0, \pi]]$

$\therefore \quad x=\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}\quad\ldots[\because x \in[0, \pi]]$

$\therefore \quad$ Required number of roots are 4.

content: Let $2 \sin ^2 x+3 \sin x-2>0$ and $x^2-x-2<0$. ( $x$ is measured in radians). The $x$ lies in the interval
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & 2 \sin ^2 x+3 \sin x-2>0 \\ & \text { Let } y=\sin x \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Equation (i) becomes } \\ & 2 y^2+3 y-2>0 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 y^2+4 y-y-2>0 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 y(y+2)-1(y+2)>0 \\ \therefore \quad & (2 y-1)(y+2)>0 \\ \therefore \quad & (2 y-1)>0 \text { and }(y+2)>0 \quad\text{OR}\\ & (2 y-1)<0 \text { and }(y+2)<0 \end{array}$$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & y>\frac{1}{2} \text { and } y>-2 \quad\text{OR}\\ & y<\frac{1}{2} \text { and } y<-2 \\ \therefore \quad & \sin x>\frac{1}{2} \text { and } \sin x>-2 \quad\text{OR}\\ & \sin x<\frac{1}{2} \text { and } \sin x<-2 \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad x>\frac{\pi}{6}$ and $\sin x>-2$

$\ldots[\because-1 \leq \sin x \leq 1$, second condition is not possible]

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & x>\frac{\pi}{6} \text { and } \sin x>-1 \quad \ldots[\because-1 \leq \sin x \leq 1] \\ \therefore & x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{6}, \infty\right) \ldots \text { (i) } \end{array}$$

Given that $x^2-x-2<0$

$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & (x-2)(x+1)<0 \\ \therefore & (x-2)<0 \text { and }(x+1)>0\quad\text{OR}\end{array}$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & (x-2)>0 \text { and }(x+1)<0 \\ \therefore & x<2 \text { and } x>-1 \quad\text{OR}\\ & x>2 \text { and } x<-1 \\ \therefore \quad & x \in(-1,2)\quad\text { ...(ii) }[\because \text { second condition is not possible }] \end{array}$$

$x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{6}, 2\right)$

content: If $\theta$ and $\alpha$ are not odd multiples of $\frac{\pi}{2}$ then $\tan \theta=\tan \alpha$ implies principal solution is
explanation:
The tangent function has a period of $\pi$. This means that if $\tan \theta = \tan \alpha$, then $\theta$ must differ from $\alpha$ by an integer multiple of the period $\pi$.

Thus, the principal solution to the equation $\tan \theta = \tan \alpha$ is:

$$ \theta = \alpha + n\pi, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z} $$

This corresponds to Option C:

$ \theta = n\pi + \alpha, \, n \in \mathbb{Z} $

content: The general solution of $2 \sqrt{3} \cos ^2 \theta=\sin \theta$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & 2 \sqrt{3} \cos ^2 \theta=\sin \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{3}\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)=\sin \theta \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{3} \sin ^2 \theta+\sin \theta-2 \sqrt{3}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\sqrt{3} \sin \theta+2)(2 \sin \theta-\sqrt{3})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} \sin \theta+2=0 \text { or } 2 \sin \theta-\sqrt{3}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}, \text { which is not possible } \\ & \text { or } \sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\sin \frac{\pi}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right), \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z} \end{aligned}$$

content: The principal solutions, of the equation $\sqrt{3} \sec x+2=0$, are
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{3} \sec x+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sec x=\frac{-2}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos x=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos x=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos \frac{5 \pi}{6} \\ & \text { and } \cos x=\cos \left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos \frac{7 \pi}{6} \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { The principal solutions are } \frac{5 \pi}{6} \text { and } \frac{7 \pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: The principal solutions of the equation $$\sec x+\tan x=2 \cos x$$ are
explanation:
The given equation is defined for $$x \neq \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}$$.

Now, $$\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}+\frac{1}{\cos x}=2 \cos x \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2 \cos ^2 x \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2\left(1-\sin ^2 x\right) \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2(1-\sin x)(1+\sin x) \\ & \Rightarrow(1+\sin x)[2(1-\sin x)-1]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(1-\sin x)-1=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\ldots\left[\begin{array}{r} \because \sin x \neq-1 \text { otherwise } \cos x=0 \text { and } \\ \tan x, \sec x \text { will be undefined } \end{array}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6} \text { in }(0,2 \pi) \end{aligned}$$

content: The solution of $$\sin x+\sin 5 x=\sin 3 x$$ in $$(0, \pi / 2)$$ are
explanation:
Here, $$\sin x+\sin 5 x=\sin 3 x$$

We know that,

$$\begin{aligned} & \sin A+\sin B=2 \sin \frac{(A+B)}{2} \cos \frac{(A-B)}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & \sin x+\sin 5 x=\sin 3 x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 2 \sin \left(\frac{5 x+x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{5 x-x}{2}\right)=\sin 3 x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 2 \sin 3 x \cos 2 x=\sin 3 x \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Either, $$\sin 3 x=0$$ or $$2 \cos 2 x-1=0$$

$$\sin 3 x=\sin 0 \text { or } \cos 2 x=\frac{1}{2}=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}$$

Comparing obtained equation with standard equation, we have

$$\begin{aligned} 3 x & =n \pi \text { or } 2 x=2 m \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3} \\ \therefore \quad x & =\frac{n \pi}{3} \text { or } x=m \pi+\frac{\pi}{6}, \text { where } m, n \in Z \\ x & =\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$(1+\sqrt{1+x}) \tan x=1+\sqrt{1-x}$$, then $$\sin 4 x$$ is
explanation:
$$\because \tan x=\frac{1+\sqrt{1-x}}{1+\sqrt{1+x}}$$

Let $$x=\cos \theta$$

Then, $$\sqrt{1-\cos \theta}=\sqrt{2} \sin \frac{\theta}{2}$$

$$\sqrt{1+x}=\sqrt{2} \cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \tan x=\frac{\sqrt{2}\left|\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\sin \frac{\theta}{2}\right|}{\sqrt{2}\left|\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right|}=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{4}+\sin \frac{\theta}{2}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos \frac{\theta}{2}}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \tan x=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{8}+\frac{\theta}{4}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & 4 x=\frac{\pi}{2}+\theta \\ \Rightarrow & \sin 4 x=\cos \theta=x \end{array}$$

content: The general solution of the equation $$3 \sec ^2 \theta=2 \operatorname{cosec} \theta$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 3 \sec ^2 \theta=2 \operatorname{cosec} \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{3}{\cos ^2 \theta}=\frac{2}{\sin \theta} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{3}{1-\sin ^2 \theta}=\frac{2}{\sin \theta} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin ^2 \theta+3 \sin \theta-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(2 \sin \theta-1)(\sin \theta+2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

or $$\sin \theta=-2$$, which is not possible

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \sin \theta & =\frac{1}{2}=\sin \frac{\pi}{6} \\ \Rightarrow \theta & =\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\pi}{6}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z} \end{aligned}$$

content: The solution set of $$8 \cos ^2 \theta+14 \cos \theta+5=0$$, in the interval $$[0,2 \pi]$$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & 8 \cos ^2 \theta+14 \cos \theta+5=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 8 \cos ^2 \theta+10 \cos \theta+4 \cos \theta+5=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 \cos \theta(4 \cos \theta+5)+1(4 \cos \theta+5)=0 \\ \therefore \quad & (2 \cos \theta+1)(4 \cos \theta+5)=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{2} \text { or } \cos \theta=\frac{-5}{4} \end{array}$$

But $$\cos \theta=\frac{-5}{4}$$ is not possible as $$\cos \theta \in[-1,1]$$ for all values of $$\theta$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \theta \in\left\{\frac{2 \pi}{3}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}\right\} \end{aligned}$$

content: If general solution of $$\cos ^2 \theta-2 \sin \theta+\frac{1}{4}=0$$ is $$\theta=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{\mathrm{A}}+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\pi}{\mathrm{B}}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z}$$, then $$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}$$ has the
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \cos ^2 \theta-2 \sin \theta+\frac{1}{4}=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)-2 \sin \theta+\frac{1}{4}=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \sin ^2 \theta+2 \sin \theta-\frac{5}{4}=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 4 \sin ^2 \theta+8 \sin \theta-5=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 4 \sin ^2 \theta+10 \sin \theta-2 \sin \theta-5=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 \sin \theta(2 \sin \theta+5)-1(2 \sin \theta+5)=0 \\ \therefore \quad & (2 \sin \theta-1)(2 \sin \theta+5)=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \text { or } \sin \theta=\frac{-5}{2} \end{array}$$

But $$\sin \theta=\frac{-5}{2}$$ is not possible as $$\sin \theta \in[-1,1]$$ for all values of $$\theta$$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore & \sin \theta=\sin \frac{\pi}{6} \\ \therefore & \theta=\frac{n \pi}{1}+(-1)^n \frac{\pi}{6} \\ \therefore \quad & A=1 \text { and } B=6 \\ & \Rightarrow A+B=7 \end{array}$$

content: If $$\cos x+\cos y-\cos (x+y)=\frac{3}{2}$$, then
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \cos x+\cos y-\cos (x+y)=\frac{3}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad 2 \cos \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)-\left(2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)-1\right)=\frac{3}{2} \\ & \ldots\left[\begin{array}{l} \because \cos \alpha+\cos \beta=2 \cos \left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right) \text { and } \\ \cos \theta=2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)-1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad 2 \cos \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)-2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{2}-1 \\ & \therefore \quad 2 \cos \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)-2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad 4 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)-4 \cos \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)+1=0 \\ \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $$\cos \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=\mathrm{t}$$, we get

$$4 t^2-4 t \cos \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)+1=0$$

As $$t$$ is real, we get $$b^2-4 a c \geq 0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left[-4 \cos \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)\right]^2-4 \times 4 \times 1 \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 16 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)-16 \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2\left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right) \geq 1 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2\left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)=1 \quad ...[\because -1 \le \cos\theta \le 1, \text{for all values of} ~\theta]\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-y}{2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=y \end{aligned}$$

content: If the general solution of the equation $$\frac{\tan 3 x-1}{\tan 3 x+1}=\sqrt{3}$$ is $$x=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{\mathrm{p}}+\frac{7 \pi}{\mathrm{q}}, \mathrm{n}, \mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \in \mathrm{Z}$$, then $$\frac{p}{q}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\tan 3 x-1}{\tan 3 x+1}=\sqrt{3} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\tan 3 x-\tan \frac{\pi}{4}}{1+(\tan 3 x)\left(\tan \frac{\pi}{4}\right)}=\sqrt{3} \\ \therefore \quad & \tan \left(3 x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & 3 x-\frac{\pi}{4}=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 3 x=\mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4} \\ \therefore & 3 x=\mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{7 \pi}{12} \\ \therefore & x=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{3}+\frac{7 \pi}{36} \end{array}$$

Comparing with $$\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{\mathrm{p}}+\frac{7 \pi}{\mathrm{q}}$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{p}=3, \mathrm{q} & =36 \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{q}}=\frac{3}{36} & =\frac{1}{12} \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of possible solutions of $$\sin \theta+\sin 4 \theta+\sin 7 \theta=0, \theta \in(0, \pi)$$ are
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \sin 7 \theta+\sin \theta+\sin 4 \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin 4 \theta \cos 3 \theta+\sin 4 \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta(2 \cos 3 \theta+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta=0 \text { or } \cos 3 \theta=\frac{-1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta=0 \text { or } \cos 3 \theta=\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \theta=n \pi \quad \text { or } 3 \theta=2 n \pi \pm \frac{2 \pi}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{n \pi}{4} \quad \text { or } \quad \theta=\frac{2 n \pi}{3} \pm \frac{2 \pi}{9} \\ \therefore & \quad \theta=\frac{2 \pi}{9}, \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{4 \pi}{9}, \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{8 \pi}{9} \cdots[\because \theta \in(0, \pi)] \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Number of solutions }=6 \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of solutions of $$\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x$$ in $$[0,2 \pi]$$ are
explanation:
The given equation is defined for $$x \neq \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}$$.

Now, $$\tan x+\sec x=2 \cos x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}+\frac{1}{\cos x}=2 \cos x \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2 \cos ^2 x \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2\left(1-\sin ^2 x\right) \\ & \Rightarrow(\sin x+1)=2(1-\sin x)(1+\sin x) \\ & \Rightarrow(1+\sin x)[2(1-\sin x)-1]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(1-\sin x)-1=0 \\ & \quad \ldots\left[\begin{array}{r} \because \sin x \neq-1 \text { otherwise } \cos x=0 \text { and } \\ \tan x, \sec x \text { will be undefined } \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6} \text { in }(0,2 \pi) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Number of solutions = 2

content: The number of solutions in $$[0,2 \pi]$$ of the equation $$16^{\sin ^2 x}+16^{\cos ^2 x}=10$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 16^{\sin ^2 x}+16^{\cos ^2 x}=10 \\ & 16^{\sin ^2 x}+16^{1-\sin ^2 x}=10 \\ & 16^{\sin ^2 x}+\frac{16}{16^{\sin ^2 x}}=10 \end{aligned}$$

Let $$16^{\sin ^2 x}=t$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad \mathrm{t}+\frac{16}{\mathrm{t}}=10 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{t}^2-10 \mathrm{t}+16=0 \\ \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=2 \text { and } \mathrm{t}=8 \end{array}$$

Now, $$16^{\sin ^2 x}=2$$ and $$16^{\sin ^2 x}=8$$

$$2^{4 \sin ^2 x}=2^1 \text { and } 2^{4 \sin ^2 x}=2^3 $$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 4 \sin ^2 x=1 \text { and } 4 \sin ^2 x=3 \\ \therefore & \sin ^2 x=\frac{1}{4} \text { and } \sin ^2 x=\frac{3}{4} \\ & \sin x= \pm \frac{1}{2} \text { and } \sin x= \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \therefore & x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}, \frac{7 \pi}{6}, \frac{11 \pi}{6} \text { and } x=\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2 \pi}{3}, \frac{4 \pi}{3}, \frac{5 \pi}{3} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ number of solutions $$=8$$.

content: The principal solutions of $$\cot x=\sqrt{3}$$ are
explanation:
To determine the principal solutions of the equation $$\cot x = \sqrt{3}$$, we start by recalling the definition and properties of the cotangent function. The cotangent function, $$\cot x$$, is the reciprocal of the tangent function, so we have:

$$ \cot x = \frac{1}{\tan x} $$

Given that $$\cot x = \sqrt{3}$$, we can rewrite this as:

$$ \frac{1}{\tan x} = \sqrt{3} $$

This implies that:

$$ \tan x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} $$

To find the angles whose tangent is $$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$$, we recall the values of the tangent function for standard angles. The value $$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$$ corresponds to the angle of $$\frac{\pi}{6}$$.

Since the tangent function repeats every $$\pi$$, the general solutions for the equation $$\tan x = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$$ are:

$$ x = \frac{\pi}{6} + n\pi $$

where $$n$$ is an integer.

For the principal solutions, we consider the interval $$[0, 2\pi)$$. The values that fall in this interval are:

$$ x = \frac{\pi}{6} \quad \text{and} \quad x = \frac{7\pi}{6} $$

Therefore, the principal solutions of the equation $$\cot x = \sqrt{3}$$ are:

$$\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{7 \pi}{6}$$

The correct answer is:

Option C

$$ \frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{7 \pi}{6} $$

content: If $$3 \sin \theta=2 \sin 3 \theta$$ and $$0< \theta<\pi$$, then $$\sin \theta=$$
explanation:
We can use the triple angle formula for sine: $$ \sin 3 \theta = 3 \sin \theta - 4 \sin^3 \theta $$. Substituting this into the given equation, we get:

$$3 \sin \theta = 2 (3 \sin \theta - 4 \sin^3 \theta)$$

Simplifying, we get:

$$3 \sin \theta = 6 \sin \theta - 8 \sin^3 \theta$$

$$8 \sin^3 \theta - 3 \sin \theta = 0$$

$$\sin \theta (8 \sin^2 \theta - 3) = 0$$

This gives us two possibilities:

$$\sin \theta = 0$$ or

$$8 \sin^2 \theta - 3 = 0$$

If $$\sin \theta = 0$$, then $$\theta = 0$$ or $$\theta = \pi$$, but these values are outside the given interval $$0 < \theta < \pi$$.

So we must have $$8 \sin^2 \theta - 3 = 0$$. Solving for $$\sin \theta$$, we get:

$$\sin^2 \theta = \frac{3}{8}$$

$$\sin \theta = \pm \sqrt{\frac{3}{8}} = \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{2}}$$

Since $$0 < \theta < \pi$$, $$\sin \theta$$ is positive. Therefore, $$\sin \theta = \boxed{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{2}}}$$.

content: If $$\theta+\phi=\alpha$$ and $$\tan \theta=k \tan \phi($$ where $$K>1)$$, then the value of $$\sin (\theta-\phi)$$ is
explanation:
We have $$\tan \theta=\mathrm{k} \tan \phi$$ and $$\theta+\phi=\alpha$$

$$\therefore \frac{\tan \theta}{\tan \phi}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{1}$$

By Componendo Dividendo, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\tan \theta+\tan \phi}{\tan \theta-\tan \phi}=\frac{\mathrm{k}+1}{\mathrm{k}-1} \\ & \therefore \frac{\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}+\frac{\sin \phi}{\cos \phi}}{\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}-\frac{\mathrm{sin} \phi}{\cos \phi}}=\frac{\mathrm{k}+1}{\mathrm{k}-1} \\ & \therefore \frac{\sin \cos \phi+\cos \theta \sin \phi}{\sin \theta \cos \phi-\cos \theta \sin \phi}=\frac{\mathrm{k}+1}{\mathrm{k}-1} \\ & \therefore \frac{\sin (\theta+\phi)}{\sin (\theta-\phi)}=\frac{\mathrm{k}+1}{\mathrm{k}-1} \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \alpha}{\sin (\theta-\phi)}=\frac{\mathrm{k}+1}{\mathrm{k}-1} \\ & \therefore \sin (\theta-\phi)=\frac{\mathrm{k}-1}{\mathrm{k}+1}(\sin \alpha) \end{aligned}$$

content: $$2 \sin \left(\theta+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\cos \left(\theta-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$, then $$\tan \theta=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & 2 \sin \left(\theta+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\cos \left(\theta-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \\ & 2\left[\sin \theta \cos \frac{\pi}{3}+\cos \theta \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right]=\left(\cos \theta \cos \frac{\pi}{6}+\sin \theta \sin \frac{\pi}{6}\right) \\ & \therefore 2\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3} \cos \theta}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3} \cos \theta}{2}+\frac{\sin \theta}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3} \cos \theta}{2}\right)=0 \Rightarrow \sin \theta+\sqrt{3} \cos \theta=0 \Rightarrow \tan \theta=-\sqrt{3}\end{aligned}$$

content: The principal solutions of $$\sqrt{3} \sec x+2=0$$ are
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{3} \sec x+2=0 \\ & \therefore \sec x=\frac{-2}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \cos x=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \therefore \cos x=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos \left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \Rightarrow x=\frac{5 \pi}{6}, \frac{7 \pi}{6}\end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ and $$x$$ satisfies the equation $$\sin x \cos x=\frac{1}{4}$$, then the values of $$x$$ are
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \sin x \cos x=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \therefore 2 \sin x \cos x=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \sin 2 x=\frac{1}{2}=\sin 30^{\circ} \\ & \therefore 2 x=30^{\circ}=\frac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{12} \\ & \therefore x=\frac{\pi}{2} \pm \frac{\pi}{12} \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{12} \text { or } x=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{12} \\ & \therefore x=\frac{7 \pi}{12} \text { or } \frac{5 \pi}{12} \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{12}, \frac{5 \pi}{12}\quad ....[\because x\in\left(0,\frac{\pi}{2}\right)]\end{aligned}$$

content: If $2 \cos ^2 \theta+3 \cos \theta=2$, then permissible value of $\cos \theta$ is
explanation: $$\begin{array}{l} \text { We have, } 2 \cos ^2 \theta+3 \cos \theta=2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 2 \cos ^2 \theta+3 \cos \theta-2=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 2 \cos ^2 \theta+4 \cos \theta-\cos \theta-2=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad (2 \cos \theta-1)(\cos \theta+2)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 2 \cos \theta-1=0, \cos \theta+2 \neq 0 \\ \therefore \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \end{array}$$

content: The principal solutions of $\cot x=\sqrt{3}$ are
explanation:

$\therefore$ Principal solution is $\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{7 \pi}{6}$

content: $$\tan A+2 \tan 2 A+4 \tan 4 A+8 \cot 8 A=$$
explanation:
We have,

$$\tan A+2 \tan 2 A+4 \tan 4 A+8 \cot 8 A$$

We know that,

$$\cot A-\tan A=2 \cot 2 A$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \tan A=\cot A-2 \cot 2 A \\ & =\cot A-2 \cot 2 A+2 \tan 2 A+4 \tan 4 A+8 \cot 8 A \\ & =\cot A-4 \cot 4 A+4 \tan 4 A+8 \cot 8 A+8 \cot 8 A=\cot A \end{aligned}$$

content: The values of $x$ in $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ satisfying the equation $\sin x \cos x=\frac{1}{4}$ are ..........
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { We have, } \sin x \cos x=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin x \cos x=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \sin 2 x=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad 2 x=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6} \\ \Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{\pi}{12} \\ \text { If } x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right), \text { then } x=\frac{\pi}{12}, \frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{12}=\frac{\pi}{12}, \frac{5 \pi}{12} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$
